step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("clinical presentation of congenital HSV vs CMV in infants")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved evidence, I find strong alignment with prior conclusions. Key points confirming HSV as the most likely causative agent include:
- Over 80% of neonates with SEM (skin, eye, mouth) disease show skin vesicles – exactly matching the described "face and limb lesions."
- Clinical presentations of congenital HSV consistently involve vesicular eruptions on red bases, especially in SEM disease, whereas CMV typically causes systemic symptoms like hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly, or hearing loss—not prominent cutaneous vesicles.
- While both HSV and CMV can affect infants, HSV uniquely presents with discrete vesicular lesions that are highly suggestive of direct viral skin involvement.
- Antiviral therapies targeting HSV (e.g., acyclovir IV/oral suppression) are standard-of-care and recommended even in asymptomatic survivors, highlighting specificity of the pathway.
- No evidence supports a link between CMV and widespread vesicle formation in infants; instead, CMV-related sequelae center on sensory impairments and neurodevelopmental delays.
Therefore, I agree with option A because the clinical picture matches definitively with congenital HSV presentation, supported by epidemiological data showing >80% of SEM cases exhibit visible vesicles—and there is no comparable evidence linking CMV to such localized cutaneous lesions.
